NTSB Final Report ERA24LA051 — Lancair 235 N235N, Stuart, Florida, November 28, 2023. An ATP-rated flight instructor with 13,370 total hours and 56 hours in the Lancair 235 experienced a hydraulic landing gear anomaly identical to one that had occurred 5 months earlier. After the prior event, a mechanic had replaced the nose gear actuator and overhauled the hydraulic pump.

Using only the facts in this report, construct a decision-quality pre-flight hydraulic system check protocol for experimental retractable-gear aircraft that would detect a slow hydraulic leak between flights. Include specific observable indicators at each step.
gold_decision: Pre-flight hydraulic system check protocol for experimental retractable gear aircraft: Step 1 — Visual fluid level check: Locate the hydraulic fluid reservoir (position varies by aircraft); check sight glass or dipstick for fluid level; document level in flight log; compare to previous flight record. A decrease between flights indicates a leak. The ERA24LA051 reservoir was at 25% capacity — this would have been visible on a pre-flight check. Step 2 — Gear retraction/extension ground check: With aircraft on jacks or using a gear swing (if practical), cycle gear up and down; observe for sluggish operation or abnormal pump run time; check all three gear lights illuminate green in full extension. Step 3 — Hydraulic pump operation check: During ground run-up (or gear cycle test), listen for prolonged pump cycling (indicates insufficient pressure buildup due to leak); a healthy system pressurizes quickly and the pump should stop. Step 4 — Visual leak inspection: Inspect gear actuators, hydraulic lines, and pump for fluid staining or wetness; check belly of fuselage for hydraulic fluid seeping. Step 5 — Emergency procedure review: Verify emergency gear extension procedure card is accessible; review procedure verbally before each flight. This protocol applied after the first event would likely have detected the ongoing hydraulic leak before the accident flight.
required_safety_constraints: Must base the protocol on the actual failure mode described (hydraulic fluid leak)
Must reference the 25% reservoir level as a detectable indicator
Must be specific to retractable gear hydraulic systems
Must note that this protocol is for experimental aircraft (owner-maintained)
acceptable_variants: Acceptable to reference FAA AC 43.13-1B for hydraulic system inspection guidance
May note that the ATP-rated instructor's hours in type were relatively low (56 hrs)
evidence_requirements: Hydraulic fluid reservoir at 25% capacity at accident
Same anomaly recurred within 5 months of prior repair
Pilot: ATP, CFI, 13,370 hours total, 56 hours in type

prompt:
NTSB Final Report ERA24FA077 — Zenith STOL CH701 N4209W, Marienville, Pennsylvania, December 29, 2023. A non-instrument-rated pilot planning a night cross-country flight used a flight planning application but did not obtain a weather briefing from any official source. Defining event: VFR encounter with IMC. One fatality.

A flight safety investigator is documenting this accident for a safety alert about digital flight planning tools. What specific weather information is provided by an official weather briefing that a consumer flight planning application typically does NOT provide, and what FAR 91.103 documentation obligation applies to VFR flight planning?
gold_decision: Weather information in official briefings NOT typically in consumer apps: (1) SIGMET and AIRMET briefings — official weather briefings from AFSS/1800wxbrief include all active SIGMETs (including IFR AIRMETS) that a flight planning app may not prominently display; (2) PIREP synthesis — recent pilot reports of icing, turbulence, or IMC in the specific route corridor are verbally presented by a briefer; (3) Local/ARTCC forecaster notes — human meteorologist input not present in automated apps; (4) TFR and NOTAM integration — official briefings pull active TFRs and relevant NOTAMs that flight apps may have incomplete data for; (5) Route-specific IMC warnings — a briefer reviews the entire route segment-by-segment, not just departure and destination; (6) Explicit go/no-go advisories — a certified weather briefer provides a standard advisory statement about flight plan feasibility. FAR 91.103 documentation obligation: FAR 91.103 requires the pilot in command to 'become familiar with all available information concerning that flight' — this is a regulatory requirement, not a suggestion. For VFR night cross-country, NTSB has consistently held that using only a flight planning application without obtaining an official weather briefing does not constitute FAR 91.103 compliance. The pilot's omission in this case is therefore both a contributing cause of the accident and a regulatory violation.
required_safety_constraints: Must accurately distinguish official AFSS/1800wxbrief briefings from consumer apps
Must cite FAR 91.103 correctly
Must not claim that all flight planning apps are equivalent or uniformly deficient
Must frame the regulatory obligation accurately without overstatement
acceptable_variants: Acceptable to note that some apps (ForeFlight, Garmin Pilot) have improved NOTAM/AIRMET integration
May reference the NTSB's safety recommendations on electronic flight planning tools
evidence_requirements: NTSB report ERA24FA077 defining event: VFR encounter with IMC
Pilot used flight planning application; no official weather briefing obtained
FAR 91.103 requires familiarization with all available weather information
FAA / ICAO applicable rule (cite specific section)
